story:
This place was crowded with Echoes—not the hollow return of a shout, but presences you could feel if you stilled your mind, sometimes even see. They weren’t ghosts, whatever a passing band of strangers had once insisted. Ghosts sounded bitter, grasping. Echoes were gentle, familiar, dear—like a soft blanket, a cool breeze, a mother’s steady hug from a time you could barely remember. She often wondered if those images were true memories or something her mind had taught itself to create.

Echoes were free of all the slow indignities of flesh and time. They did not ache or fade. They were light, joyful, and patient, guiding the attentive through a world that had come unstitched. You had to be perceptive to notice them. Few people were. Few people were left.

In this later age—no one kept calendars anymore—populations had fallen off a cliff. Roads were broken and empty. Cities had flattened into hard, wild plains. Money meant nothing; there was no ledger left to hold it. What used to be knowledge had been hunted down and burned: books, libraries, newsrooms, computers—deemed subversive by a small, angry knot of enforcers who silenced any thought that did not mirror their own. Resistance splintered into countless factions until the makeshift power they fought over collapsed under its own weight. After that final reckoning, the survivors slipped backward into wildness—half feral, untaught, untrusting. She did not know why she had kept her words and her gentleness when so many had lost theirs. The Echoes saved her. They stayed, and she learned to live with the empty distances and the present moment—because the present was all that still belonged to her.

It was in that present that she noticed a new Echo gathering in a field of dry weeds dusted with fine gray ash. She had stopped there because it looked harmless enough and, without meaning to, slept harder than she could remember ever sleeping. Morning came soft and mild. She woke foggy and unsure of how she had arrived. A small dust devil rose, lifting ash and sunlight into quick, vanishing spirals. As the vortices thinned, she quieted herself, and the Echo of a deep forest began to bloom—thick and steady—until it wrapped all her senses.

It felt real. The shadows were not shadows but a living wood. Far above, birds moved in a sky-darkening tide—hundreds, then thousands. She had no memory of birds in flight—any bird at all—and the sight and sound of so many made her ache with wonder. Had wings truly once carried such vast, purposeful journeys?

The air turned cool and resinous with cedar and pine, a damp green fog that chilled her skin. Sunlight sifted through the canopy, pointing her toward a footpath that winked in and out between ancient trunks. She followed. Moss and leaf mulch softened her steps until she seemed to glide, the path unfurling into a new journey, away from the harsh and the unpredictable. Where fear had been, calm settled.

The trees opened onto a meadow: deep grass sown with tiny flowers in every shade. Beyond, water hurried unseen. At the far edge, the woods rose again, old and inviting. Drawn forward, she crossed into their shadows and let them take her, merging with the forest—willingly becoming an Echo of herself and of everything she had once known.
